metadata.working_title: A SUMO E3 ligase promotes long non-coding RNA transcription to regulate small RNA-directed DNA elimination

abstract_sketch.problem: Small RNAs target complementary chromatin regions for silencing via nascent long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs), but how the genome is transcribed to communicate with small RNAs during programmed DNA elimination is unclear.
abstract_sketch.gap: In Tetrahymena, parental macronuclear lncRNAs (pMAC-lncRNAs) interact with scnRNAs to trigger target-directed small RNA degradation (TDSD), yet the factors that promote pMAC-lncRNA transcription are not identified.
abstract_sketch.approach: We characterized Ema2 (TTHERM_00113330), a conjugation-specific SUMO E3 ligase with an atypical SP-RING domain, using knockout analysis, mass spectrometry, cell fractionation and site-directed mutagenesis of Spt6 lysine residues.
abstract_sketch.key_results: Ema2 is required for DNA elimination, TDSD, pMAC-lncRNA accumulation, H3K27me3 deposition in the parental MAC, and chromatin association of Spt6 and RNA polymerase II. Ema2 interacts with the SUMO E2 Ubc9, catalyzes Spt6 SUMOylation, and high molecular weight (>200 kDa) SUMO-conjugated proteins are reduced to approximately 50% in EMA2 knockout crosses.
abstract_sketch.impact: Ema2-directed SUMOylation actively promotes lncRNA transcription as a prerequisite for small RNA-directed chromatin communication, revealing a distinctive evolutionary solution linking SUMOylation to small RNA-directed DNA elimination.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Small RNAs loaded into Argonaute proteins target chromatin through nascent lncRNAs, creating a paradox where silenced regions must be transcribed.
Fission yeast siRNAs deposit H3K9me2/3 at centromeric repeats, while Rhino in Drosophila and SHH1 in plants recruit dedicated transcription machinery for heterochromatin lncRNAs.
In ciliates, source and target loci of small RNAs reside in different nuclei, providing a unique model to dissect lncRNA functions.
Tetrahymena programmed DNA elimination downsizes the 200 Mb MIC genome to the 103 Mb MAC genome by removing ~12,000 internal elimination sequences (IESs).
Three types of lncRNAs (MIC-lncRNA, pMAC-lncRNA, nMAC-lncRNA) regulate DNA elimination; scnRNAs interact with pMAC-lncRNAs to trigger TDSD via the RNA helicase Ema1.
This study identifies the SUMO E3 ligase Ema2 as required for pMAC-lncRNA transcription and dissects its molecular mechanism via Spt6 SUMOylation.

subsections.title: Ema2 is exclusively expressed during conjugation and localized to the MAC
content_bullets: EMA2 (TTHERM_00113330) mRNA is expressed only during conjugation (Figure 1A, based on Miao et al 2009 data).
Ema2-HA is undetectable in vegetative cells and first appears in the parental MAC at 3 and 6 hpm; at 8 hpm it relocates to the new MAC and fades by 12-14 hpm (Figure 1B).

subsections.title: Ema2 is required for completing DNA elimination
content_bullets: DNA-FISH with Tlr1-complementary probes at 36 hpm shows Tlr1 only in MICs of wild-type exconjugants (Figure 2A, WT) but in both MIC and new MAC of EMA2 KO exconjugants (Figure 2A, KO).
FISH signal intensity in new MACs of EMA2 KO is lower than TWI1 KO, indicating partial DNA elimination block (Figure 2B).
EMA2 KO cells produce no viable sexual progeny (Figure 2C).

subsections.title: Ema2 is required for target-directed small RNA degradation (TDSD)
content_bullets: Northern blot with Mi-9 probe: MDS-complementary scnRNAs are detected at 3 hpm, reduced at 4.5 hpm, and undetectable at 6 hpm in wild type, but remain at 6 hpm in EMA2 KO (Figure 3A).

Small RNA sequencing of 26-32 nt reads mapped to MDS vs IES tiles at 3 and 8 hpm shows MDS-complementary scnRNAs are only slightly reduced in EMA2 KO vs strong reduction in wild type (Figure 3B, left).
IES-complementary scnRNAs are comparable at 3 and 8 hpm across all strains (Figure 3B, right), consistent with TDSD targeting only MDS-complementary scnRNAs.
The EMA2 KO TDSD defect is milder than EMA1 KO, suggesting Ema1 and Ema2 act differently.

subsections.title: Ema2 is required for the accumulation of SUMOylated proteins during conjugation
content_bullets: Ema2 contains an atypical SP-RING domain where the first zinc-binding cysteine is replaced by histidine and some stabilizer residues are missing (Figure 4A).
GST-pull down shows GST-Ema2 interacts directly with His-Ubc9, the Tetrahymena SUMO E2 enzyme (Figure 4B).

HA-Smt3 is expressed in EMA2 KO strains and Western blotting at 4.5 and 6 hpm detects high molecular weight (mainly >200 kDa) SUMOylated proteins in the WT cross; these are reduced to approximately 50% in the EMA2 KO cross (Figure 4C).
HA-Smt3 rescues essential SMT3 function (Figure EV1).

subsections.title: Ema2 is required for SUMOylation of Spt6
content_bullets: Mass spectrometry of His-Smt3-conjugated proteins excluding proteins with log2 LFQ score above 25 or more than 6 consecutive histidine residues; Spt6 is the most abundantly detected target in the WT cross and is strongly reduced in the EMA2 KO cross (Figure 5A).
Western blot of HA-tagged Spt6 at 4.5 and 6 hpm shows a slower-migrating modified Spt6 band in the WT cross that is largely absent in the EMA2 KO cross (Figure 5B).
Anti-HA immunoprecipitation followed by anti-Smt3 blotting confirms the slower-migrating species is SUMOylated Spt6 and that Ema2 is needed for the majority of Spt6 SUMOylation (Figure 5C).

subsections.title: Ema2 is required for pMAC-lncRNA accumulation
content_bullets: Nested RT-PCR targeting MAC M, L8 and R2 IES-MDS boundary loci detects pMAC-lncRNAs in wild-type but not EMA2 KO cells at 6 hpm; RPL21 mRNA positive control is detected in both (Figure 6A).
Genomic PCR with the same primers confirms the MAC loci are present in EMA2 KO cells (Figure EV3).

J2 antibody immunofluorescence detects long dsRNAs in the MIC at 3 hpm, parental MAC at 6 hpm, and new MAC at 9 hpm in WT; in EMA2 KO, parental MAC dsRNA is undetectable at 6 hpm while MIC and new MAC signals are preserved (Figure 6B).
EMA1 KO retains parental MAC dsRNA, supporting that Ema1 is needed for scnRNA interaction rather than lncRNA transcription.
H3K27me3 is undetectable in the parental MAC of EMA2, EMA1, TWI1 and EZL1 KO cells at 6 hpm (Figure 6C).

subsections.title: Ema2 facilitates chromatin association of Spt6 and RNA polymerase II
content_bullets: Immunofluorescence shows Spt6-HA and Rpb3 are localized to the MAC at 4.5 hpm in both WT and EMA2 KO crosses (Figure 7A).

Cell fractionation at 4.5 hpm separates soluble (S1) and chromatin-bound (S2) proteins; Spt6-HA and Rpb3 are in both fractions in the WT cross, with SUMOylated Spt6 exclusively in the chromatin fraction.
In the EMA2 KO cross, Spt6-HA and Rpb3 in the chromatin (S2) fraction are greatly reduced (Figure 7B).
Spt6 and RNAPII remain insoluble after RNase A treatment while Twi1 (which binds chromatin via nascent lncRNA) is released, indicating chromatin association is not RNA-dependent (Figure 7C).
Findings validated in replicated experiments (Figures EV5, EV6).

subsections.title: Spt6 SUMOylation itself is dispensable for pMAC-lncRNA transcription
content_bullets: Spt6 is divided into an N-terminal domain of low complexity (DOL) and conserved TEX-like and SH2 domains; K-to-R mutants targeting all DOL lysines (DOL-KR) and surface lysines predicted by JASSA in N, M, and C regions were constructed (Figure 8A).
All constructs rescue SPT6 KO lethality (Figure EV7); HA-SPT6-N-KR and M-KR exhibit meiotic and mating defects, while HA-SPT6-DOL-KR and C-KR progress through conjugation.
HA-Spt6-DOL-KR is SUMOylated like wild type; HA-Spt6-C-KR shows no detectable SUMOylation (Figure 8B).

In HA-SPT6-C-KR cells: Spt6 and RNAPII remain in the chromatin-bound fraction (Figure 8C), long dsRNAs accumulate in the parental MAC (Figure 8D), but exconjugants show a mild DNA elimination defect (Figure 8E).
Conclusion: Spt6 SUMOylation per se is not required for pMAC-lncRNA transcription, but plays an additional role in DNA elimination.

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Ema2 facilitates genome-wide pMAC-lncRNA transcription as a prerequisite for scnRNA-chromatin communication and TDSD.
Because Spt6 and RNAPII are essential for vegetative viability but Ema2 is conjugation-specific, the loss of chromatin Spt6/RNAPII must be a temporal event during the mid-conjugation window.
Ema2 may maintain Spt6 and RNAPII on chromatin under a transiently altered chromatin environment, or commit excess transcription machinery (upregulated during conjugation) to chromatin.

Spt6-C-KR rules out Spt6 SUMOylation as the direct driver of lncRNA transcription; other Ema2-dependent substrates (possibly undetected due to degradation or competition with other lysine modifications) may be involved.

Unlike fission yeast Swi6/Chp2/Clr4, C. elegans HDAC1, and Drosophila Su(var)2-10/Panx SUMOylation (which act on existing lncRNAs), Ema2 acts upstream of lncRNA transcription; heterochromatin is not a prerequisite for lncRNA transcription in Tetrahymena, suggesting a distinctive evolutionary solution.

sections.title: Materials and Methods
content_bullets: Tetrahymena strains B2086, CU427, CU428, B*VI, B*VII from the Tetrahymena stock center; EMA2 KO strains from Shehzada & Mochizuki 2022.
SPP medium with 2% proteose peptone at 30 degC; conjugation by mixing pre-starved cells of two mating types in 10 mM Tris pH 7.5 at ~5-7 x 10^5/mL.

Antibodies: anti-H3K27me3 (Millipore 07-449), anti-HA HA11 (Covance 6B12), anti-H3K9me3 (Active Motif 39162), anti-GST (BD 554805), anti-His (Proteintech 66005-1-Ig), anti-long dsRNA J2 (Jena Bioscience), anti-alpha-tubulin 12G10, anti-Rpb3 (Kataoka & Mochizuki 2017), anti-Smt3 from James Forney (Nasir et al 2015).
DNA-FISH by nick-translated Cy5 probes from pMBR 4C1, pMBR 2 and Tlr IntB plasmids; IES retention index uses a correction factor of 0.35 so TWI1 KO cells average 1.
Viability test: mating pairs isolated in 1x SPP drops at 6 hpm, incubated ~3 days, then examined for 6-methylpurine resistance and paromomycin sensitivity.
Small RNA analysis: 10 ug total RNA separated on 15% acrylamide-urea gel; small RNAs mapped as 26- to 32-nt reads to MDS and IES tiles (RPKM).
GST pull-down: E. coli codon-optimized EMA2 and UBC9 cloned into pGEX-4T-1 and pET28a; His-Ubc9 expression induced with 0.05 mM IPTG for ~16 h at 25 degC.
His-Smt3 purification: lysis in 6 M guanidine-HCl, 93.2 mM Na2HPO4, 6.8 mM NaH2PO4, 10 mM Tris pH 8.0; Ni-NTA elution with 6 M urea, 58 mM Na2HPO4, 42 mM NaH2PO4, 500 mM imidazole.
HA-Smt3 purification: TCA precipitation, dissolve in SDS, dilute in 20 mM Tris pH 7.5, 100 mM NaCl, 2 mM MgCl2, 2 mM CaCl2, incubate with anti-HA beads.

Cell fractionation (Ali et al 2018): 1.4 x 10^6 cells lysed in 50 mM Tris pH 8, 100 mM NaCl, 5 mM MgCl2, 1 mM EDTA, 0.5% Triton X-100; supernatant (S1) and sonicated solubilized chromatin (S2); RNase A variant uses 20 ug/mL.
Nested RT-PCR of MAC M, L8, R2 IES boundary loci using primers listed in Supplementary Information.
